replies.author: @OopseaDoopsea
replies.text:
​@PrebleStreetRecords I don't think the reason for these stupid scenes is done out of laziness or the incompetence of the director/writers. I think these are deliberate choices to add suspense and add to the tone of the show. This isn't Band of Brothers or John Wick, this is a show about a group of teenagers fighting off a monster invasion. If the show runners went with a "realistic" approach then this show would have been done by the first season and every major cast member would likely be dead. Part of the problem is they have to choose between creating an action packed scene while also handling what amounts to a dozen or more extras on set. Honestly, it wouldn't make sense for them to invest the time and effort into showing a realistic military scenario because that's NOT the focus of the scene at all. The scene is supposed to be chaotic while the main cast attempts to get out of the absolute clusterfuck they are caught in. If I were in the position of the director, knowing what I know, I would have gone the same route, because in the end it doesn't matter how the extras or soliders handle themselves in that scene, it's how the main characters get from point A to B and how it culminates in Will's manifestation of his powers.

Yes, it's bothersome to see David Harbor not know how to handle a gun, and it's frustrating to see this trope played out in almost every single hollywood production in regards to weapon handling. It's frustrating to see how when Hopper shoots at the soliders in the Upside Down from an extremely compromised position. It's frustrating to see the soliders do the dumbest shit imaginable. They don't take cover, they don't flank the enemy position, they don't coordinate with each other as you'd expect from trained soliders. But here's the thing. If they DID do what was expected, then either Hopper would be dead or they'd have to invest way more time and effort into making Harbor seem like a believable solider...which might just not be in the cards for him or in the budget for the show. The scenes with soliders are meant to be tense and you have to suspend your disbelief for a moment to make it work. In real life, soliders taking fire from a concentrated position would not move forward slowly like lemmings, they would not stand in the open, they would not allow themselves to be overwhelmed by a single enemy. Hopper SHOULD be dead, but the plot needs him to continue, so the compromise is to make the soliders seem dumber than most NPC's you'd see in a modern video game.

Believe me, I get it. I see it all the time in shows like this, but you have to remember the context of the show. This isn't Extraction or John Wick...both of which have brilliant gun/fight choreography. This is a show about teenagers fighting monsters. 

The only points where i can really fault the writers is the deliberate attempts to make unlikeable characters into something they aren't. Erica is an arrogant bitch with zero redeeming qualities. Robin is nothing more than a talking head meant to tick off the LGBT box even though her best moments in season 3 were when there was obvious sexual tension between her and Steve (they had great chemistry and it would have been believable for them to become an item, but they made her gay at the last moment as some kind of twist that honestly threw me for a loop the first time I saw it). Will's mom is basically useless and just getting in the way but she's here to stay so they have to do something with her. The inclusion of Eleven's sister is the most baffling because she comes from one of the worst/most forgettable episodes of the show.

I'm glad that Will is finally paying off as a character and I honestly hope his arc is satisfying at the end like it was by the end of episode 4. I can overlook the unrealistic depictions of military forces because I understand why those choices were made, but I can't forgive the attempts to make terrible characters into the main focus of the show.

replies.text:
ST was a moderately fun show that hit at the early part of the 80's revival in culture that lasted about a decade or so. Just like the show has tapered off in quality, as some of the interesting quirks and mystery got old, it's end roughly coincides with the tapering popularity of the 80's vibe.

Oh and comparing this series to Game of Thrones is a sour joke. I know GoT ended on a bad note, but it gave us about 4-5 seasons of absolute peak television and character building. And most of the 6th and some of that 7th season was still better than the best that Stranger Things has ever done. Even in popularity, I thiink the only reason Stranger Things got so big, because everybody had Netflix anyway, so it was easily accesible.
GoT was still at the heights of downloading, which was even accepted by the showrunners as a positive, as it basically built up the name of HBO and resulted in many new subscriptions through the sheer hype built by people trying it for free and word of mouth.

Funnily enough, with streaming services on a massive downturn, Netflix certainly among them, we are again starting to see massive increases in streaming from less legal sources. People are certainly willing to pay for entertainment, but the services that are available are no longer worth the money, so they resort to more affordable and free sources once again. Things keep coming full circle after all.

replies.text:
Which is why I keep saying it should've been an anthology. Just imagine how much more bone-chilling it would be if the end of S1's story were that final shot with Will, how mysterious and uncertain it would make the ending. It's a classic horror trope. Everything seems fine, but it's not; the monster will always be there.

Then we follow another story of people dealing with an entirely new monster from the Upside Down, completely different from the demegorgan but equally as strange. It could even take place in a completely new location with a new set of characters, naming it after something else, and for the same reason the demegorgan was named: because it was the only way they could understand it or even frame what was happening.

Honestly that has been an aspect all along, the weirdness that the demogorgons more resistant to weaponry and guns but vulnerable to melee weapons. 

Think it has to do with the fact it takes more effort, more 'willpower' to use a melee weapon vs a gun. A way to put it, when facing a demogorgon you are sorta accidentally using a bit of psychic power to use 'smite' on the monster to bybpass its physical defense. Your will vs the mdinflayer's will that is keeping that tiny aspect of itself together and Vecna conducting it all.


A strong enough will can be used to slay a demogorgon easily. a weaker will struggles. And guns don't conduct the will well cause they are relying on the gun to do the damage, not themselves. 


Way to put it, humans naturally have a bit of psychic field going on and don't know how to conduct it normally.

replies.author: @dewulfe9913
replies.text:
I don't disagree - the whole point of Season 4's finale was basically "Hell has come to Earth!!" ...and that hasn't been reflected (eg. in the overall tone, in the mood of the Hawkins residents etc) like it should have been. The writers went into end-game territory and then as Drinker says, just walked it all back?!?!

That said, Vecna did get just about killed in Season 4, so potentially he's had to take a long time to heal up and reform (a la Sauron after the Ring was cut from his finger). 

But even then, the problem I see, is the execution of that idea by the producer/writers. Where in the past I think it was more common to chronologically track what the main characters (including villain) were doing, now the 'BIG REVEAL' is favoured. 

So instead of at least getting hints that Vecna was damaged and was slowly doing things to regain his strength, what we get is the kids doing busy-work for 3.5 episodes, and then suddenly Vecna walks into the final set piece as though he never got lit up at all. It's not that far off "Somehow Palpatine returned" level storytelling, and it sucks.

replies.text:
Nearly 10 years overall to wrap this thing up is a travesty. For comparison, Star Trek Deep Space 9 released each season sequentially. They had a Season start in 09/1993, end in 06/1994, and start a new Season in 09/1994. These are big seasons too, with almost 26 episodes each season and next to no wait time.

What exactly are they doing here? Why was there a THREE YEAR gap between S4 and 5?

As to Will - I felt the same way. This happened when people interpreted him saying that he wasn't interested in girls in Season 2 as him being gay, when it's more likely that a kid who lived through two traumatic events probably just wanted to be a kid and act like a kid. He's rejecting growing up, compared to his friends, and it could have been an interesting dig into how kids deal with trauma. Instead, nope, he's gay. In the 80's.

​@jmcbastard9665 It has nothing to do with a gay character simply being in the show. The issue is that these characters are usually products of lazy writing done to get brownie points from a specific group of people. Will's plot just sorta came out of nowhere and felt very out of character for him. Robin's relationship was and continues to feel out of place in the grand scheme of things, demanding time taken away from the main plot solely to say "Look! it exists!"
If I were gay, and my most frequent form of representation was through lazy writing with the most bare minimum importance, often seemed like the concept as a whole was cast aside and only done for the sake of representation, I would be pretty bothered. That's not to say there aren't shows that do gay characters right. I've seen dozens that make a gay relationship flow into the story as naturally as a straight relationship. Stranger Things just... isn't one of them.

author: @Strikerage
text:
People are blaming the latest episode getting review bombed because of homophobia.

It isn't because people are "homophobes". Trust me... Gays think their identity is the center of the world. No one honestly cares. We get that there's pressure with coming out in the 90s and oughts, but no one cares anymore.

About the episode, the coming out scene just didn't make any sense. The previous half of the season ended with him coming to terms with himself and who he is. It was insinuated that he didn't care what others thought. That's what gave him the self confidence to reach his full power. That made sense....

But the writers had to get more gay storylines in there, because at the end of the day, in a world where the universe is collapsing, of course the most important thing to gays is their identity... They need to waste precious time on themselves and their feelings, when they could be enacting a plan to save the world. The narcissism is oblivious to everyone who actually cares about his sexuality.

The bad reviews are just the rest of the audience refusing to be treated like idiots. At the end of the day, no one cares where Will wants to put his junk. We just don't think it even matters to the storyline.

I can't wait until my comment is down voted by the gay mafia. They are the reason Hollywood is gonna be replaced by AI. And it can't come sooner.
